preFix.title: Sealing the gap between window frame and wall
preFix.subtitle: Foam rod backing and flexible sealant at the frame-to-masonry junction
preFix.excerpt: The gap where a window frame meets the masonry reveal is one of the most draughty junctions in an older house. Mortar cracks and paint bridges do not seal it: they break with thermal movement. This is the sequence for clearing the old sealant, sizing and fitting a foam rod backer, and applying flexible sealant that will last 10 to 15 years.
preFix.sourceNotes: NHBC Standards 6.7 on window frame sealing requirements. BRE Good Building Guide 41 on airtightness detailing at window frames. BS 6093:2006 Design of joints and jointing in building construction.

content.text: The perimeter of a window frame moves by 3 to 6 mm across the annual temperature range. Rigid fillers crack within a few years of installation. The correct filler is a flexible sealant backed by a foam backer rod that acts as a movement joint. This detail takes 30 to 60 minutes per window and seals most of the frame-to-wall air leakage in one visit.
Foam backer rod, 10 mm diameter for gaps up to 8 mm, 15 mm for wider gaps.
Low-modulus silicone or polyurethane sealant in a colour matching the frame or wall.
Small hook or screwdriver for raking out old filler.
Rake out all old filler from the joint: mortar, mastic, and foam. The joint must be clean, dry, and open before the backer rod goes in. Old mastic prevents adhesion of the new sealant.
Measure the joint width at several points. Choose a backer rod diameter 20 to 30% larger than the joint width: the rod should be slightly compressed when pushed in. Not forced in.
Push the backer rod into the joint to a depth of about 5 mm below the surface. Using a rounded tool to avoid piercing the rod. The rod sits behind the sealant, not mixed into it.
Apply masking tape to both sides of the joint, 2 mm back from the edge. This gives a clean line and prevents sealant smearing onto the frame paint or the render.

Cut the sealant nozzle at a 45-degree angle to a 6 mm opening. Apply a continuous bead along the joint. Pressing the gun smoothly and filling the full depth from the backer rod to the surface. Do not stop and start mid-run.

Tool the sealant with a wetted finger or a spatula. Pressing it into the joint and forming a slightly concave profile. This profile is important: a convex bead peels at the edges as the joint moves. While a concave or flat bead stays bonded.
Remove the masking tape while the sealant is still wet. Leave to cure for 24 hours before painting over with a paint compatible with the sealant type.

The exterior seal prevents rain and wind entering the joint. The interior seal prevents warm moist air from the house reaching cold surfaces in the wall construction. Both are needed for a proper result. If budget only allows one. Prioritise the exterior seal to prevent water damage; the interior seal can follow next dry season.
High-modulus sealant was used instead of low-modulus. Replace with a flexible low-modulus silicone or polyurethane. The joint is a movement joint; rigidity is the enemy.

attrs.symptom: Sealant pulls away from masonry but not from frame

content.text: The masonry surface had dust or release agent on it. Wire-brush the reveal to clean masonry, wipe with a damp cloth, let it dry. And re-prime with a silicone primer before re-sealing.

initialFindings.message: tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
